---
title: Fan-out/Fan-in
description: Process multiple items in parallel and collect results
---

When you have a large batch of independent work to do, processing items one by one is slow. The fan-out/fan-in pattern splits work into parallel tasks, processes them simultaneously, then collects all the results.

This pattern dramatically reduces processing time for bulk operations while maintaining reliability through Restate's durable execution.

## The problem fan-out/fan-in solves

Imagine you need to:
- Validate 1000 user email addresses
- Process 500 uploaded images  
- Send notifications to 200 users
- Fetch data from 50 different APIs

Processing these sequentially could take hours. Fan-out/fan-in processes them in parallel, reducing total time to minutes.

## How fan-out/fan-in works

**Fan-out phase:** Split your batch into individual tasks and start them all simultaneously.

**Parallel processing:** Each task runs independently. Failures in one task don't affect others.

**Fan-in phase:** Collect results from all tasks, handling both successes and failures appropriately.

**Fan-out/Fan-in handlers:**
<CodeGroup>
```typescript TypeScript
export const processUserEmails = async (ctx: WorkflowContext, userIds: string[]) => {
  // Fan-out: Start all email processing tasks in parallel
  const emailTasks = userIds.map(userId => 
    ctx.serviceCall(EmailService).validateAndSendWelcome(userId)
  );
  
  // Fan-in: Wait for all tasks to complete
  const results = await Promise.allSettled(emailTasks);
  
  // Process results
  const successful: string[] = [];
  const failed: Array<{userId: string, error: string}> = [];
  
  results.forEach((result, index) => {
    const userId = userIds[index];
    if (result.status === 'fulfilled') {
      successful.push(userId);
    } else {
      failed.push({ userId, error: result.reason.message });
    }
  });
  
  return {
    processed: userIds.length,
    successful: successful.length,
    failed: failed.length,
    failedUsers: failed
  };
};

// Alternative: Process with progress tracking
export const processWithProgress = async (ctx: WorkflowContext, items: string[]) => {
  const results: Array<{id: string, status: 'success' | 'error', data?: any, error?: string}> = [];
  
  // Process in batches to avoid overwhelming downstream services
  const batchSize = 10;
  for (let i = 0; i < items.length; i += batchSize) {
    const batch = items.slice(i, i + batchSize);
    
    // Fan-out this batch
    const batchTasks = batch.map(async (item) => {
      try {
        const result = await ctx.run(`process-${item}`, () => ProcessingService.process(item));
        return { id: item, status: 'success' as const, data: result };
      } catch (error) {
        return { id: item, status: 'error' as const, error: error.message };
      }
    });
    
    // Fan-in this batch
    const batchResults = await Promise.all(batchTasks);
    results.push(...batchResults);
    
    // Store progress
    ctx.set('progress', { completed: results.length, total: items.length });
  }
  
  return results;
};
```

```java Java
@Workflow
public BatchResult processUserEmails(WorkflowContext ctx, List<String> userIds) {
    // Fan-out: Start all email processing tasks in parallel
    List<Awaitable<String>> emailTasks = userIds.stream()
        .map(userId -> EmailServiceClient.fromContext(ctx).validateAndSendWelcome(userId))
        .collect(Collectors.toList());
    
    // Fan-in: Wait for all tasks to complete
    List<Try<String>> results = Awaitable.all(emailTasks).await();
    
    // Process results
    List<String> successful = new ArrayList<>();
    List<FailedUser> failed = new ArrayList<>();
    
    for (int i = 0; i < results.size(); i++) {
        String userId = userIds.get(i);
        Try<String> result = results.get(i);
        
        if (result.isSuccess()) {
            successful.add(userId);
        } else {
            failed.add(new FailedUser(userId, result.getCause().getMessage()));
        }
    }
    
    return new BatchResult(userIds.size(), successful.size(), failed.size(), failed);
}

// Batch processing variant
@Workflow  
public List<ProcessingResult> processWithBatches(WorkflowContext ctx, List<String> items) {
    List<ProcessingResult> allResults = new ArrayList<>();
    int batchSize = 10;
    
    for (int i = 0; i < items.size(); i += batchSize) {
        List<String> batch = items.subList(i, Math.min(i + batchSize, items.size()));
        
        // Fan-out this batch
        List<Awaitable<ProcessingResult>> batchTasks = batch.stream()
            .map(item -> ctx.run("process-" + item, () -> {
                try {
                    Object result = ProcessingServiceClient.fromContext(ctx).process(item).await();
                    return new ProcessingResult(item, "success", result, null);
                } catch (Exception error) {
                    return new ProcessingResult(item, "error", null, error.getMessage());
                }
            }))
            .collect(Collectors.toList());
        
        // Fan-in this batch
        List<ProcessingResult> batchResults = Awaitable.all(batchTasks).await();
        allResults.addAll(batchResults);
        
        // Store progress
        ctx.set("progress", new Progress(allResults.size(), items.size()));
    }
    
    return allResults;
}
```

```python Python
async def process_user_emails(ctx: WorkflowContext, user_ids: List[str]):
    # Fan-out: Start all email processing tasks in parallel
    email_tasks = [
        EmailService.from_context(ctx).validate_and_send_welcome(user_id)
        for user_id in user_ids
    ]
    
    # Fan-in: Wait for all tasks to complete
    results = await asyncio.gather(*email_tasks, return_exceptions=True)
    
    # Process results
    successful = []
    failed = []
    
    for i, result in enumerate(results):
        user_id = user_ids[i]
        if isinstance(result, Exception):
            failed.append({"user_id": user_id, "error": str(result)})
        else:
            successful.append(user_id)
    
    return {
        "processed": len(user_ids),
        "successful": len(successful),
        "failed": len(failed),
        "failed_users": failed
    }

# Batch processing variant
async def process_with_batches(ctx: WorkflowContext, items: List[str]):
    results = []
    batch_size = 10
    
    for i in range(0, len(items), batch_size):
        batch = items[i:i + batch_size]
        
        # Fan-out this batch
        async def process_item(item):
            try:
                result = await ctx.run(f"process-{item}", 
                    lambda: ProcessingService.from_context(ctx).process(item))
                return {"id": item, "status": "success", "data": result}
            except Exception as error:
                return {"id": item, "status": "error", "error": str(error)}
        
        batch_tasks = [process_item(item) for item in batch]
        
        # Fan-in this batch
        batch_results = await asyncio.gather(*batch_tasks)
        results.extend(batch_results)
        
        # Store progress
        await ctx.set("progress", {"completed": len(results), "total": len(items)})
    
    return results
```
</CodeGroup>

**Invoking fan-out/fan-in workflows:**
<CodeGroup>
```bash cURL
curl localhost:8080/BatchProcessor/batch-123/processUserEmails \
  --json '{"userIds": ["user1", "user2", "user3", "user4", "user5"]}'
```

```typescript TypeScript Client
const result = await restateClient
  .workflowClient(BatchProcessor, "batch-123")
  .processUserEmails(["user1", "user2", "user3", "user4", "user5"]);
```

```java Java Client
BatchResult result = BatchProcessorClient.fromContext(ctx, "batch-123")
    .processUserEmails(userIds).await();
```
</CodeGroup>

## Key implementation patterns

**Promise.allSettled() for fault tolerance:** Unlike `Promise.all()`, `allSettled()` waits for all tasks to complete, whether they succeed or fail. This prevents one failure from canceling other work.

**Batch processing for large datasets:** Processing 10,000 items simultaneously might overwhelm downstream services. Break large batches into smaller chunks.

**Progress tracking:** For long-running batches, store progress in state so users can track completion and resume if needed.

**Error handling strategies:** Decide whether to fail fast (stop on first error) or collect all results (continue despite errors). Most fan-out scenarios want to collect all results.

## Fan-out strategies

**Simple fan-out:** Start all tasks immediately
```typescript
const tasks = items.map(item => processItem(item));
const results = await Promise.allSettled(tasks);
```

**Batched fan-out:** Process in smaller groups to control resource usage
```typescript
for (let i = 0; i < items.length; i += batchSize) {
  const batch = items.slice(i, i + batchSize);
  // Process batch...
}
```

**Throttled fan-out:** Add delays between starting tasks
```typescript
const tasks = items.map(async (item, index) => {
  await ctx.sleep(index * 100); // Stagger by 100ms
  return processItem(item);
});
```

## Common use cases

**Data validation:** Validate thousands of records in parallel
- Input sanitization for bulk imports
- Email address validation
- Credit card verification

**Content processing:** Transform media or documents
- Image resizing and optimization  
- PDF generation from templates
- Audio/video transcoding

**API aggregation:** Fetch data from multiple sources
- Collect user data from different services
- Aggregate metrics from multiple systems
- Parallel third-party API calls

**Notification delivery:** Send messages to many recipients
- Email campaigns to subscriber lists
- Push notifications to mobile devices
- SMS alerts to user groups

## Performance considerations

**Downstream capacity:** Don't overwhelm target services. Use batching or throttling if needed.

**Memory usage:** Large result sets consume memory. Consider streaming results or processing in chunks.

**Failure rates:** If many tasks fail, investigate root causes rather than retrying blindly.

**Resource limits:** Monitor CPU, memory, and network usage during large fan-out operations.
